candidate.name: mtkwifi apcli_connect command substitution injection
candidate.input: HTTP form fields copied into cfgs[k] in apcli_cfg(), including ApCliSsid and ApCliWPAPSK
processing_chain: POST to apcli_cfg handler under admin/mtk/wifi
for k,v in pairs(http.formvalue()) do cfgs[k] = v end
subsequent request to apcli_connect() loads cfgs from profile
Lua os.execute invokes /bin/sh -c, so $() and backticks inside double quotes are expanded
candidate.impact: Authenticated command injection as root via Wi-Fi client configuration values

review.accept_reason: Code-level review confirms that apcli_cfg() stores attacker-controlled form values and apcli_connect() later executes them through os.execute() with insufficient escaping. __handleSpecialChars() escapes only backslash and double quote, leaving shell command substitution intact.
review.exploit_idea: Set ApCliSsid or ApCliWPAPSK to $(touch /tmp/pwned) in the admin Wi-Fi client configuration flow, then trigger apcli_connect() so the shell expands the payload before iwpriv runs.
review.notes: This is a stronger case than the earlier generic mtkwifi.lua heuristic because the source, storage step, and executable sink are all visible in code.

candidate.name: mtkwifi submit_dpp_uri direct os.execute injection
processing_chain: POST to submit_dpp_uri handler under admin/mtk/multi_ap
Lua os.execute invokes /bin/sh -c, so shell metacharacters in uri are evaluated
candidate.impact: Authenticated command injection as root via EasyMesh DPP URI submission

review.accept_reason: submit_dpp_uri() reads the uri form value and concatenates it directly into an os.execute() command with no escaping or allowlisting.
review.exploit_idea: Submit a crafted uri containing command substitution such as $(touch /tmp/dpp_pwned) to the EasyMesh DPP endpoint and observe shell execution when submit_dpp_uri() runs.
review.notes: This is the cleanest direct injection sink in the current X6000R firmware review.

candidate.name: d.cgi config endpoint to popen heuristic
candidate.input: Initial automated heuristic around d.cgi and popen references
processing_chain: HTTP request to embedded web UI
candidate.impact: Superseded heuristic; not the final validated path

review.reject_reason: Later manual reversing showed the important issue was not a generic /config flow but a hidden authenticated diagnostic interface gated by a static parameter check. This heuristic should not be counted as a separate vulnerability.
review.notes: Keep only as provenance for the automated triage stage; superseded by the confirmed hidden d.cgi entry below.

candidate.name: system.lua administration path to /bin/sh
candidate.input: HTTP administration form fields consumed by LuCI system.lua handlers
candidate.impact: Potential web command injection in administration / upgrade-related flows

review.exploit_idea: Trace exact parameter usage in system.lua and the shell command template behind the administration / applyreboot flow.
review.notes: Manual follow-up indicates the visible execution paths are mostly fixed fork_exec templates, so this remains a weak lead rather than a confirmed command-execution case.

candidate.name: hidden diagnostic d.cgi cmd parameter to popen
candidate.input: HTTP GET parameters `aaksjdkfj` and `cmd` after auth-protected CGI entry
processing_chain: get_value(...,"cmd",...) copies attacker-controlled command into stack buffer
popen(command,"r") executes via /bin/sh -c and CGI prints output in `<pre>` block
candidate.impact: Authenticated command execution via hidden diagnostic CGI

review.accept_reason: Manual disassembly confirms a direct command-execution chain in d.cgi: after auth, CSRF, and a static magic-parameter check, attacker-controlled cmd is concatenated with ` 2>&1` and passed to `popen(...,"r")`. The CGI then returns command output to the client.
review.exploit_idea: Send an authenticated request to the hidden diagnostic CGI with the static gate and a chosen command; the handler executes it with popen and prints the output in the response body.
review.notes: `14.234` `timepro.cgi` contains a near-identical predecessor path; `15.330` no longer exposes the same visible d.cgi interface, making this a useful version-diff case.

candidate.name: formWsc peer PIN command injection
candidate.input: HTTP POST parameter `peerRptPin` in older naming, observed as build-variant `peerPin` in the 2021-03-02 image
processing_chain: parameter fetch in boa form parser
command string built with `echo %s > /var/wps_peer_pin` and `iwpriv ... pin=%s` templates
candidate.impact: Command execution through WPS configuration handling

review.accept_reason: Manual report review and build revalidation both show the WPS PIN parameter reaching shell-backed command templates in boa without robust server-side sanitization.
review.exploit_idea: Use shell metacharacters in the WPS PIN parameter so the command template around `/var/wps_peer_pin` or `iwpriv ... pin=` is reinterpreted by the shell.
review.notes: The exact parameter name differs by build (`peerRptPin` vs `peerPin`), but the root issue is stable: attacker-controlled WPS PIN data is inserted into shell command templates.

candidate.name: repeater site-survey SSID command injection
candidate.input: Externally controlled SSID carried from site-survey selection into internal profile rebuild logic
processing_chain: site-survey result selected in web UI
SSID stored through internal MIB path
profile reconstruction reads the SSID back
command string built with `iwpriv %s set_mib ap_profile_add="%s",...`
candidate.sink: system() in boa repeater profile path
candidate.impact: Command execution through repeater configuration using attacker-controlled wireless environment data

review.accept_reason: Manual review confirmed a complete external-input-to-shell chain from attacker-controlled SSID into the repeater profile command template executed by boa.
review.exploit_idea: Present a malicious SSID so that when the administrator selects it in the repeater UI, the SSID is later embedded into the `ap_profile_add` shell command template.
review.notes: This is a useful recurrence case because the input source is not just a direct HTTP parameter but external wireless environment data.

candidate.name: formUploadFile filename command injection
processing_chain: command string built with `echo %s >/var/web/fw/%s/version` and related templates
candidate.impact: Command execution through firmware upload filename handling

review.accept_reason: Manual report review and later build revalidation both show multipart filename data flowing into shell command construction in boa without robust escaping.
review.exploit_idea: Place shell metacharacters in the multipart filename so the generated version-file command line is reinterpreted by the shell.
review.notes: The same vulnerable command-construction style recurs across multiple A3002RU handlers, making this a strong vendor-pattern example rather than an isolated bug.
